episode_title: Ezra Eeman: Navigating the AI Frontier at the Dutch Public Broadcaster NPO

codes.description: NPO's structure as umbrella over 13 independent broadcasters creates both representational strength and strategic coordination challenges for AI adoption
excerpts: the NPO is is actually a big umbrella organization that holds together thirteen public broadcasters
coming up with an AI strategy is is somewhat of a of a puzzle
some of our newsrooms And organizations are more than a thousand people, and others are sixty
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: A structured methodology for building AI strategy by mapping use cases across both the editorial value chain and public service mission goals
excerpts: We started building what I call a strategy rocket, which is a a way to kind of structure elements of a strategy and set a destination So that we can also tie it to our public mission.
that quickly helps to go from a a pile of possibilities, a pile of experiments, to more structured map and grid almost of use cases
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: AI strategy must serve defined public service goals -- accessibility, universality, relevance, societal representation -- not just efficiency
excerpts: We have a a very defined public service media mission that's being of public value to everyone in the Netherlands
we have to make sure that it's accessible, that it's universal, that it's very much based on on those topics and teams that are relevant for the Dutch society
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Organizing AI use cases along the editorial workflow (sourcing, production, distribution) as a common language across organizations of different sizes
excerpts: where they are in the value chain in terms of are they they're somewhere at the beginning from the sourcing or were they more in the production phase or more in the distribution side of things
that that workflow just going from the the first source to the the final distribution is quite recognizable
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Putting an 'innovation' label on AI experiments grants organizational permission to take risks that would be blocked in regular operations
excerpts: if you put a big sticker innovation on it, you're more allowed to do things than that If you have to do it in in your kind of regular work
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Most existing AI applications at NPO are production-oriented (transcription, metadata, editing) rather than audience-facing
excerpts: n not so many front end uh facing or consumer facing or audience facing experiments
A a lot in the production, I would say, um, using uh AI for transcription of podcasts. uh transcription of video labeling of metadata
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Current AI uses speed up existing processes but are not transformative -- efficiency gains rather than paradigm shifts
excerpts: the most of the things I said so far were uh preparing, brainstorming, helping in production and making that more efficient, which is the the easy, the low hanging fruits. They're not they're not necessarily transformative.
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Audience-facing AI applications are centered on accessibility rather than content generation -- easy language news, visual podcasts, synthetic voices for reading articles
excerpts: If we are currently looking into audience-facing products, these are very much centered around accessibility or expanding our offer to more more audiences
if there's one thing that language models can do very well is give you language alternatives for complex sentences
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Using LLMs to simplify complex news bulletin language for audiences who cannot follow standard political/journalistic vocabulary
excerpts: They have um an ID or a kind of Part of their work is called News in the Easy Language
the kind of preparation like how could I rephrase this in an easier way, how could I explain this word in an easy way, that's something that uh certainly a language uh tool can really speed up
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Using AI's multimodal capabilities to make audio content accessible to people with hearing disabilities through visual narrative
excerpts: the multimodality of AI is quite interesting for accessibility. How do you make people that have auditive disabilities listen to a podcast
We're experimenting with uh visual enhancements of podcasts and making that a part of the narrative is generated as an image that tells a story of a podcast
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Careful, case-by-case approach to synthetic voices with ethical deliberation (family consent, intent alignment) distinguishing powerful storytelling from exploitation
excerpts: we synthesized the voice of that investigative journalist who was not longer there. So we had a discussion with the family

it's not something that you can repeat so easily. We had a few campaigns here in in Belgium where I live, where a dead politician was actually used to do a campaign for his party. And that was less was less appreciated
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Using synthesized versions of known broadcaster voices rather than generic ones to maintain audience trust when converting text to audio
excerpts: rather than using a generic voice that they might not recognize or trust, why don't you use your own voices?
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: AI avatars enabling resource-constrained stations to produce local content (weather forecasts) that would otherwise be impossible
excerpts: in Switzerland the regional TV stations are using avatars to do regional weather forecasts because th they don't have the resources and the people to to make make those bulletins for every region
through the use of AI avatars they've been able to scale which normally would be just a map on a website
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: NPO blocks AI crawlers from training on their content as a default defensive posture in the absence of any dialogue with tech companies
excerpts: we we have agreed at the public broadcaster level that we Don't want creepers to use our content to train the models
without any conversation and on the conditions, you can not agree to it, I think, because you don't know how it will be used
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: The paradox of contributing high-quality content to AI training: absence means poor-quality models, but participation may make journalism obsolete
excerpts: if we are absent of the training model, the data and the training model, then then in the end it will only be fed by uh poor quality information
if we improve the model to that point that we're not necessary anymore, you might also dig in your own grave
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Major AI companies have not approached most European public broadcasters for partnership, focusing only on large English-speaking markets
excerpts: Maybe except for BBC and maybe our colleagues in France or Germany, there's no public broadcasters as approach in the whole of Europe
They look at the big markets. Europe for them is difficult. It's fragmented. It's many languages.
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Exploring a locally-built Dutch large language model involving cultural institutions, government, and news providers as an alternative to Silicon Valley models
excerpts: we are of course also in the Dutch market also looking into the possibility of a a more specific own Dutch large language model
if we participate we can also shape the conditions we are contributing to the quality of it and making sure it actually understands the nuances of the Dutch language and it's less of a black box
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Being part of building an LLM means you can justify and explain how it works, unlike opaque commercial models
excerpts: we really know and we can uh justify if we would use that model, we can justify how it was built and we can explain it, which is not the case if we use a kind of Silicon Valley infused tech model
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Using organizations like EBU to create a unified European voice in negotiations with tech companies rather than approaching individually
excerpts: we need to have a more European dialogue. That's why we are so invested in working together with European broadcasters and the the EBU plays a a big role
we're talking on behalf of the European broadcasting industry or at least on behalf of the public service broadcasts, which is uh a better conversation starter
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Principle that news organizations should differentiate on content/voice but collaborate on technology infrastructure
excerpts: let's compete on on content but collaborate on technology because I feel in the end why people choose us is because we We have a certain relationship, we have a certain voice and view, not necessarily because our AI model was so specifically different than our competitor
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Traditional 3-5 year strategic plans are obsolete; AI requires continuous re-evaluation and agile pivots
excerpts: I d really don't believe that the idea that you make a three to five year plan and then rest back and watch how it will unfold, that's out of the window
You you need a much more agile way of to yeah evaluate just and then kind of pivot sometimes your strategy
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: The convergence of real-time processing, multimodality, and mobile AI chips as the next transformation frontier
excerpts: the combination of multimodality, real-time and mobile. So that that triangle of everything moving to personal devices, AI chips that run on smartphones
I could speak Dutch now with you and you would hear it as English. And I could look like I'm Donald Trump and you wouldn't know.
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: AI democratizing filmmaking by allowing ideas to become moving images directly from text, unlocking a new generation of creators
excerpts: you feel the potential of the keyboard being your new camera and whatever you write can come to life
I really think that will unlock a whole new generation of people that have IDs to visualize those IDs just from their heads to something that becomes a moving image
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Fundamental shift from search engines returning ranked lists to AI providing synthesized answers, requiring different optimization strategies
excerpts: Search is already evolving. It becomes something that's much more a conversation and you don't expect any more a list of results, you expect an answer
in a search result you want to be optimized to be first in the lists while if it's an answer then you will have to optimize to be part of the answer
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: As generative search interfaces become less reliable, high-quality direct-destination news platforms may become more valuable
excerpts: if these news kind of generative interfaces are becoming yeah more used but maybe less reliable then uh certain destinations will become more valuable
our destinations if we keep them quality very high they will become even more attractive to a certain group of the audience
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Need for generative AI interfaces to use trust signals or public value signals to pick up reliable information
excerpts: we also have to make sure that those generative interfaces somehow can use trust signals or public value signals to pick up the right kind of information
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Envisioning interactive media where audiences converse with content rather than passively consuming it
excerpts: what would a conversational podcast look like. So you rather than listening to a podcast, you have a conversation with a podcast
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Despite desire for sovereignty, European broadcasters remain deeply reliant on American tech companies for cloud, compute, and software
excerpts: we're so reliant on the American tech companies. Not only Because of the the audience facing platforms that they they run, but also for our infrastructure from cloud service to computational power and and and softwares
it's a bit unthinkable that we step away completely from them
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Personal use of AI for condensing, structuring, and titling newsletter content, though it fails at higher-order reasoning like meaningful topic clustering
excerpts: I do use it to really come with j with a shorter versions of something I've already written or c more compact and concise ways to put things
it didn't really do do a very good job in uh putting the right things together, making meaningful clusters and also bridging from one topic to the other
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Unresolved questions about fair intellectual property conditions for AI-generated content based on copyrighted material
excerpts: What's the basis of those generated images? And how do we make sure that they're fairly that they're based on a kind of fair user, kind of fairly agreed IP conditions?
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

codes.description: Before strategic coordination, AI experimentation at NPO was scattered, unconnected, and not tied to overarching goals
excerpts: the first thing was really trying to understand what is everybody doing? How are we actually exploring this? It was very scattered, it was not directly connected to overarching goals
codes.speaker: Ezra Eeman

episode_summary: Eeman's account of building AI strategy at NPO reveals a central insight: the organizational challenge of AI adoption in public media is fundamentally a governance problem, not a technology one. Managing 13 independent broadcasters of vastly different sizes requires a strategic framework (the 'strategy rocket') that maps technology possibilities against public mission obligations -- a level of institutional coordination that most private media organizations do not face. The episode surfaces a profound strategic dilemma unique to public broadcasters: they must simultaneously defend their content from AI crawlers (because no terms have been negotiated) while recognizing that their absence from training data could degrade the information ecosystem they exist to serve. Eeman's distinction between competing on content and collaborating on technology offers a pragmatic philosophy, but the European dependency on American infrastructure means this collaboration occurs on fundamentally unequal terms. Most revealing is the gap between the transformative possibilities Eeman envisions (conversational podcasts, real-time translation, mobile AI) and the current reality of incremental production efficiencies, suggesting public broadcasting is in an extended bridging period between AI aspiration and AI transformation.
surprises: Despite NPO being a major European public broadcaster, no major AI company has approached them for partnership conversations

The most ethically celebrated synthetic voice use case -- resurrecting a dead journalist's voice for a JFK podcast -- required family consent and is explicitly described as not easily repeatable, contradicting the idea that synthetic voice use cases can be standardized
Eeman frames AI storyboarding as liberating for filmmakers ('keyboard as camera') despite being a former filmmaker himself aware of the IP issues this raises
The 'innovation' label functions as organizational permission to experiment -- suggesting that without strategic framing, AI work might be blocked by institutional risk aversion
LLMs fail at the higher-order reasoning task of clustering related news topics, a limitation Eeman discovered through personal newsletter production
tensions: Blocking AI crawlers protects content rights but may result in AI models trained only on poor-quality information, potentially harming the public interest the broadcaster exists to serve
NPO wants digital sovereignty through a Dutch LLM but remains deeply dependent on American cloud, compute, and software infrastructure with no realistic path to full independence

AI use cases are described as 'low-hanging fruit' and 'not transformative' in the present, yet the strategic vision invokes radical transformation (conversational podcasts, real-time translation) -- with no clear bridge between the two states
The decentralized structure that makes NPO democratically representative also makes unified AI strategy extremely difficult to implement
Eeman celebrates AI's potential to democratize filmmaking while simultaneously worrying about unresolved IP conditions for the generated content
